coredata.dc:title: A dual-emitting fluoroprobe fabricated by aloe leaf-based N-doped carbon quantum dots and copper nanoclusters for nitenpyram detection in waters by virtue of inner filter effect and static quenching principles
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Junxia
Sun, Yueying
Wang, Panpan
Sun, Zhengpeng
Wang, Yawei
Gao, Ming
Wang, Huili
Wang, Xuedong
coredata.dc:description:
Fluorescence sensing technique has been used in environmental analysis due to its simplicity, low cost, and visualization. Although the fruit pulp-based biomass carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have excellent luminescent properties, aloe leaves possess the superiority of being easily accessible in all seasons compared to fruit pulp. Thus, we fabricated Aloe carazo leaf-based nitrogen doping-CQDs (N-CQDs) using a facile hydrothermal approach, which emitted bright blue fluorescence with a quantum yield of 21.4 %. By comparison, the glutathione-encapsulated copper nanoclusters (GSH-CuNCs) displayed strong red fluorescence. A blue/red dual emission based on the N-CQDs/CuNCs mixture was established for nitenpyram detection. At the 350-nm excitation, the N-CQD/CuNCs system produced dual-wavelength emitting peaks at 440 and 660 nm, respectively. Moreover, when nitenpyram was introduced into the system, the fluorescence intensities (FIs) of N-CQDs significantly decreased, whereas the FIs of GSH-CuNCs varied slightly; simultaneously, the solution color changed from bright blue to dark red. Both the spectral overlapping between nitenpyram's UV–Vis absorption and N-CQDs’ excitation and almost unchanged fluorescence lifetimes indicated the occurrence of inner-filtering effect (IFE) in the dual-emitting fluoroprobe. In addition, the Stern-Volmer constant (K
sv = 6.92 × 103 M−1), temperature effect, as well as UV–Vis absorption of N-CQD/CuNCs before and after the addition of nitenpyram corroborated the static-quenching behavior. Consequently, the fluorescence-quenching of N-CQDs by nitenpyram was attributable to the joint IFE and static-quenching principles. A good linearity existed between the F660/F440 values and nitenpyram concentrations (0.5–200 μM) with a method detection limit of 0.15 μM. The dual-emitting fluoroprobe provided the satisfactory recoveries (95.0%–107.0 %) for nitenpyram detection in real-world waters, which were comparable with the results of traditional liquid chromatography coupled to tandem mass spectrometry method. Owing to its simple operations, low-cost, and adaptability for on-site outdoor monitoring, the newly developed dual-emitting fluoroprobe possesses great potential applications in routine monitoring of nitenpyram under field conditions.

head.ce:title: A dual-emitting fluoroprobe fabricated by aloe leaf-based N-doped carbon quantum dots and copper nanoclusters for nitenpyram detection in waters by virtue of inner filter effect and static quenching principles

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence sensing technique has been used in environmental analysis due to its simplicity, low cost, and visualization. Although the fruit pulp-based biomass carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have excellent luminescent properties, aloe leaves possess the superiority of being easily accessible in all seasons compared to fruit pulp. Thus, we fabricated Aloe carazo leaf-based nitrogen doping-CQDs (N-CQDs) using a facile hydrothermal approach, which emitted bright blue fluorescence with a quantum yield of 21.4 %. By comparison, the glutathione-encapsulated copper nanoclusters (GSH-CuNCs) displayed strong red fluorescence. A blue/red dual emission based on the N-CQDs/CuNCs mixture was established for nitenpyram detection.

At the 350-nm excitation, the N-CQD/CuNCs system produced dual-wavelength emitting peaks at 440 and 660 nm, respectively. Moreover, when nitenpyram was introduced into the system, the fluorescence intensities (FIs) of N-CQDs significantly decreased, whereas the FIs of GSH-CuNCs varied slightly; simultaneously, the solution color changed from bright blue to dark red. Both the spectral overlapping between nitenpyram's UV–Vis absorption and N-CQDs’ excitation and almost unchanged fluorescence lifetimes indicated the occurrence of inner-filtering effect (IFE) in the dual-emitting fluoroprobe. In addition, the Stern-Volmer constant (K
sv = 6.92 × 103 M−1), temperature effect, as well as UV–Vis absorption of N-CQD/CuNCs before and after the addition of nitenpyram corroborated the static-quenching behavior. Consequently, the fluorescence-quenching of N-CQDs by nitenpyram was attributable to the joint IFE and static-quenching principles. A good linearity existed between the F660/F440 values and nitenpyram concentrations (0.5–200 μM) with a method detection limit of 0.15 μM.

The dual-emitting fluoroprobe provided the satisfactory recoveries (95.0%–107.0 %) for nitenpyram detection in real-world waters, which were comparable with the results of traditional liquid chromatography coupled to tandem mass spectrometry method. Owing to its simple operations, low-cost, and adaptability for on-site outdoor monitoring, the newly developed dual-emitting fluoroprobe possesses great potential applications in routine monitoring of nitenpyram under field conditions.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the detection for nitenpyram by N-CQDs/CuNCs basing on combined static quenching effect and IFE mechanism.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: As a typical class of pesticides, neonicotinoids (NEOs) are widely used to control pests and pathogens in agricultural and forestry production [1]. Nitenpyram is a preferred kind of NEOs due to its high efficiency and no cross-resistance [2]. However, nitenpyram residues have been extensively monitored in environmental media owing to its overuse, bioaccumulation and potential toxicity [3,4]. Notably, the metabolic products of nitenpyram are stronger than their parent molecule in terms of environmental migration and persistence, which pose a great risk to ecological safety [5]. Conventional nitenpyram detection is usually based on sophisticated and expensive instruments, such as gas chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (GC-MS/MS) [4], liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (LC/MS) [6], and ultra-pressure liquid chromatography coupled to tandem mass spectrometry (UPLC-MS/MS) [7,8]. However, the time-consuming and complex pretreatment procedures of these aforementioned methods have enlightened us to develop a rapid, efficient and convenient approach for nitenpyram detection in environmental matrices.

So far, fluorescent sensors and electrochemical detection have been widely reported for on-site monitoring of NEOs based on their fast sensing for analysts and good sensitivity and selectivity [9,10]. Various types of fluorescent sensors have attracted plentiful eyes, such as metal nanoclusters (MNCs) [11,12], carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [13], and nanocomposites [11,14,15]. Compared to noble metals-based nanoclusters (AuNCs and AgNCs), copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) are of cost-effective preparation and easy achievability. In the synthetic process of CuNCs, reduced glutathione (GSH) is often used as a stable protector and functional modifier, which can effectively enhance photo-stability, hinder from aggregation and increase dispersion [16]. CQDs, especially for the biomass-based CQDs, are emerging as promising fluoroprobes owing to their high signal read-out intensity, facile preparation, and low cost [17,18]. Several types of CQDs were synthesized by virtue of common natural sugars (glucose, soluble starch, fructose or sucrose), natural citric acid derivatives, fresh vegetables (tomatoes, potatoes) and fruits (bayberry, orange, loquat, lemon juice) as raw materials [19,20]. The structures and luminescent properties of CQDs are dependent on the synthetic conditions, and the fluorescence quantum yields (QYs) of CQDs can be optimized by atomic doping, such as N, P, S and so on [21]. Especially, nitrogen-doping can effectively improve the chemical structures and fluorescent properties of CQDs by means of enhancing electron density of adjacent carbon atoms, forming a new radiation pathway, and adjusting energy band structure [22]. Rostami et al. developed a fluorescent sensor of nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) @SiO2 for nitenpyram detection, exhibited remarkable sensing selectivity and sensitivity among four similar molecules and six metal ions. Nitenpyram absorbed the excitation light of this sensor basing on the inner filter effect [23]. The previously developed fluorescent sensors were mainly focused on single signal for the detection of analytes. However, the accuracy and sensitivity with the single read-out signal were susceptible to interference by light source intensity, environmental factor, probe concentration, and intrinsic fluorescence. Ratiometric (dual-emitting) fluoroprobes, based on dual read-out signals, can overcome the disadvantages of single signal [24–26], because quantitative assay was based on the ratiometric relationship of dual signals vs. analyte concentrations.

In general, the underlying mechanisms regarding dual-emitting fluoroprobes are mainly involved in aggregation-induced emission (AIE), fluorescence-resonance energy transfer (FRET) [27], and inner-filter effect (IFE) [28]. Li and colleagues integrated CuNCs with N-doped CQDs to develop a dual-emitting fluoroprobe for Pb2+ assay in Porphyra and found that Pb2+ had the AIE effect on the fluorescent intensity of CuNCs [29]. Zhang's group fabricated the CQDs-AuNCs nanohybrids with dual read-out signals for dopamine assay, based on the FRET principle from the overlapping phenomenon between the fluorescence of CQDs (donor) with the excitation of AuNCs (acceptor) [24]. Currently, the IFE-based fluoroprobes have attracted more and more attention because of their simplicity, flexibility and without the complex linkage between the electron donor and the acceptor, and only require certain spectral overlap between the excitation of the donors and the excitation/emission of the acceptors [30]. Yan and coworkers successfully engineered and developed a CQDs/AgNCs based fluoroprobe for Cu2+ detection based on IFE phenomenon [31]. Yang et al. developed a dual-emission fluoroprobe of sulfur-doped CQDs and CuNC hybrid for sensitive and selective detection of dinotefuran based on an IFE mechanism [25]. Osman et al. devised a nanoprobe of N,S-CQDs for determining tinidazole via an IFE principle. Chen et al. constructed a ratiometric fluorescence sensing system based on N-CQDs@CuNCs complexes for the detection of thiram and paraquat, and speculated that the fluorescence quenching was dominated by electrostatic interactions [32]. With regret, a paucity of information is available on the application of N-CQDs/CuNCs as a dual-emitting fluoroprobe for nitenpyram detection, and the detailed fluorescence-quenching mechanisms remain unclear; for example, whether or not the quenching is involved in the joint action of photo-transfer pathway (AIE, FRET, IFE, and so on) and attenuation effect (static, dynamic or static-dynamic).

Informed by the previous reports and research gaps, we herein combined the aloe leaf-based N-CQDs with GSH-encapsulated CuNCs (GSH-CuNCs) to pioneer an IFE-based dual-emitting fluoroprobe for nitenpyram detection (Scheme 1 ). N-CQDs were fabricated by a facile hydrothermal approach using Aloe carazo leaves as biomass carbon source and urea as nitrogen source. The fluorescence intensities (FIs) of N-CQDs were reduced following the addition of CuNCs through an IFE phenomenon. Also, the addition of nitenpyram markedly quenched the fluorescence of N-CQDs and weakened the IFE between two fluorophores. The color in the mixed system of N-CQDs/GSH-CuNCs complexes varied from blue to red with the rising nitenpyram concentrations under UV irradiation, thereby realizing the sensitive fluorescence detection of nitenpyram. The main novelites of this study lie in three aspects: (1) Construction of the N-CQDs/GSH-CuNCs based dual-emitting fluoroprobe addresses the issues of the single read-out signal for sensitive detection of nitenpyram; (2) The quenched fluorescence of N-CQDs by nitenpyram is comprehensively confirmed to be the joint effect of IFE and static quenching by a series of spectral techniques; and (3) the utilization of waste aloe leaves in the synthesis of N-CQDs is expected to achieve the reuse of biomass, which is line with China national “dual-carbon” policy. In addition, this work opens a new avenue for nitenpyram detection and demonstrates great potential of the newly developed ratiometric sensor in one-site and outdoor monitoring in comparison with conventional instrument analysis.

All chemical reagents were of analytical grade and purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China): reduced glutathione (L-GSH), HCl, NaOH, FeCl3, CuSO4, KCl, MgCl2, Zn(NO3)2, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, AgCl, MnCl2, CaCl2, Na2CO3, Na2HPO4, citric acid (CA), and quinine sulfate (QSF). The four standards of NEOs were procured from J&K Scientific Corporation (Beijing, China), including imidacloprid, acetamiprid, clothianidin, and nitenpyram. Aloe carazo was sourced from a flower market in Suzhou, China. When received, the analytical-grade chemicals were utilized directly without any purification. Ultrapure water with electrical conductivity of ≥18.2 MΩ was produced by a Milli-Q system (Bedford, MA, USA) and employed in all experimental procedures.

For N-CQDs preparation, aloe leaves were washed in ultrapure water, dried in a freeze dryer, and then ground into powder by a QM-3SP2 planetary ball-mill machine (Nanjing University Instrument Corporation, Nanjing, China). Next, 0.6 g of aloe powder and 0.3 g of urea were uniformly dispersed in a 50-mL centrifuge tube containing 35 mL of ultrapure water. Afterwards, the mixed solution was transferred into a 100-mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, and then the autoclave was put into oven at 200 °C for 12 h. After reaction, the resulting solution was cooled to ambient temperature and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was filtered with 0.22-μm membrane filter and further dialyzed for 24 h using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight of 1000 Da. Finally, the fabricated N-CQDs was stored at 4 °C to avoid the external light until further use.

For GSH-CuNCs preparation, 10.0 mL of the L-GSH solution (50.0 mg/mL) was dropwise added to 10.0 mL of CuSO4 solution (10.0 mM) and incubated at 37 °C for 1 h. Hereafter, the above solution was reacted with 1.0 M NaOH until the milky phase turned to be transparent pale yellow, and incubated at 37 °C for 2 h [19]. The resultant GSH-CuNCs solution was stored at 4 °C for further trials.

A JEOL JSM-6700 F model SEM (Tokyo, Japan) was used to observe the morphologies of N-CQDs and GSH-CuNCs. A Bruker D8 Advance model XRD (MA, USA) was employed to characterize the crystal phase of two nanomaterials under the operational conditions of λ = 1.54178 Å and a 2θ range of 10–60°. A Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi XPS (MA, USA) was used to analyze the elemental components and their valence states. A Thermo Fisher Scientific IS5 was used to measure the FT-IR spectra of as-prepared nanomaterials. Zeta potentials of N-CQDs, CuNCs and N-CQDs/CuNCs were detected using a potential analyzer (NANO ZSE, Malvern, UK). A FS-5 spectrofluorometer was used to test the FIs and lifetimes of nanomaterials (Edinburgh, UK). The UV–vis absorption of as-prepared nanomaterials was measured using a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) at a 1-cm square quartz cell. The fluorescence imaging of the mixed system was visualized in a dark box, which was equipped with a UV lamp for 365-nm excitation. Solution pH was modulated using a Leici PBSJ-6L model pH meter (Shanghai INESA Scientific Instrument, Shanghai, China).

The stability of N-CQDs was investigated by changes in their FIs under varying buffer pH (2−12), NaCl concentrations (0–1.0 M), duration (0–120 min) under continuous 365-nm irradiation, and storage time at 4 °C. The stability of GSH-CuNCs was analyzed by detecting their 660-nm FIs at one-day intervals, and that of the mixed system of N-CQDs/GSH-CuNCs was measured by the F440/F660 values at 6-h intervals. Each test was in triplicate, and the experimental data of this study were expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD, n = 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nitenpyram detection by the dual-emitting fluoroprobe based on N-CQDs/GSH-CuNCs
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical assay, 100 μL of N-CQDs was mixed with 300 μL of GSH-CuNCs, which was then dispersed in 2.1 mL of Na2HPO4-CA buffer (pH = 3.5, 0.01 M). Next, 100 μL of nitenpyram solution at the fortification level of 0–250 μM was micropipetted into the above Na2HPO4-CA buffer. After a 10-min incubation, the FIs were measured at the emission wavelengths of 440 nm and 660 nm under the 350-nm excitation, and the excitation/emission slit widths were set as 3 nm. The ratios (F660/F440) of the FIs at 440 nm and 660 nm were calculated, and a calibration curve was constructed by plotting the F660/F440 values versus the nitenpyram concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference effect of the as-constructed dual-emitting fluoroprobe
ce:section.ce:para: For the purpose of evaluating the selectivity of the N-CQDs/CuNCs fluoroprobe, 100 μL of common ions and chemicals at 100 μM were added to the mixed system. These fortified interfering ions and chemicals included metal cations (Fe3+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, K+, Ag+), anions (Cl−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, CO3 2−), as well as similar compounds (imidacloprid, acetamiprid, and clothianidin). The F660/F440 values of the N-CQDs/CuNCs system were determined under the same conditions as described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nitenpyram detection in real-world waters by the dual-emitting fluoroprobe
ce:section.ce:para: The four surface water samples and two sewage samples were collected from the drinking water source in Taihu Lake and wastewater treatment plant in Suzhou City, respectively. The suspended particles were removed by a 10-min centrifugation at 5000 rpm and 0.45-μm membrane filtering, and the following assay procedures were similar as described in Section 2.5. The spiking recovery experiments were conducted at the three levels of nitenpyram (1, 5 and 10 μM) in real-world waters.

Simultaneously, the detected data by this fluoroprobe were compared with the results by traditional LC-MS/MS approach, and the detailed LC-MS/MS operational parameters were summarized in Supplementary materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of the synthetic conditions on the FIs of N-CQDs

ce:section.ce:para: Our research team previously synthesized the loquat fruit-based CQDs, which possessed superior fluorescence intensity and stability [19]; however, loquat fruits only mature in mid to late May each year, with a short fruit period in south China. As a seasonal fruit, they are difficult to acquire during the non-mature seasons. Moreover, the fresh loquat fruits are inconvenient to store and are prone to decay, whereas aloe leaves are available all the year and are not affected by seasonal limitation. Therefore, we used aloe leaves as a carbon source for CQDs instead of loquat fruits. As elaborated in Fig. S1a, the FIs of loquat-based CQDs increased with the increase of excitation wavelengths from 300 to 350 nm. At the 350-nm excitation wavelength, the emission intensity reached its maximum (∼1.70 × 105 a. u.) at 450 nm, and with further increasing the excitation wavelengths from 350 to 400 nm, the emission was red-shifted, and the corresponding FIs were weakened. The aloe-based CQDs displayed the similar emission spectra as the loquat-based CQDs (Fig. S1b), as reflected by the maximum emission at ∼440 nm and the slightly higher intensity (Fig. S1c). Considering easy accessibility, aloe leaves were chosen as the precursors for biomass CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of the biomass and urea dosages on the FIs of N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: At a 350-nm excitation, the 440-nm emission did not display the distinct wavelength shift with the rising dosages of aloe leaf and urea (Fig. 1 a and c). As detailed in Fig. 1b, the FIs of CQDs increased monotonically with the fortified dosages from 0.3 to 0.6 g. However, with further increases from 0.6 to 0.8 g, no marked variations in the FIs of N-CQDs were observed, which maintained at 2.85 ± 0.04 × 105 a. u. Thus, 0.6 g was selected as the appropriate fortification dosage of aloe leaf. Upon the addition of urea, the FIs of N-CQDs gradually increased with the dosages of urea increasing up to 0.3 g, which reached a plateau up to 4.88 × 105 a. u. at 0.3 g (Fig. 1d). Nevertheless, with the further rising dosages of urea (0.3–0.6 g), the FIs of N-CQDs first remained nearly unchanged (0.3–0.5 g) and then decreased (0.5–0.6 g). In summary, the optimal dosage of urea was selected to be 0.3 g.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of aloe leaf-based N-CQDs and GSH-CuNCs

ce:section.ce:para: The morphology, size, elemental composition, and surface functional groups of aloe leaf-based N-CQDs were analyzed in detail by HRTEM, XRD, FT-IR, and XPS. The TEM image indicated that N-CQDs displayed spherical shape and uniform distribution without obvious aggregation phenomenon in aqueous solution (Fig. 2 a). The HRTEM image of N-CQDs displayed the clear lattice fringes with lattice spacing of 0.23 nm (inset of Fig. 2a). The particle sizes of N-CQDs varied between 3.5 and 8.0 nm, and the average size was calculated to be 5.64 ± 0.77 nm according to the statistical data of 170 particles (Fig. 2b). XRD patterns exhibited a wide diffraction peak at 2θ = 23.74° for the amorphous carbon structure of N-CQDs (Fig. 2c) and the adjacent small peak was parallel to the (100) crystal plane spacing of graphite carbon, implying that N-CQDs had a certain degree of graphitization. For the surface functional groups using FT-IR analyses (Fig. 2d), the peak at 3230 cm−1 displayed the stretching vibration of O–H and N–H [33], while those at 2930 and 677 cm−1 represented the stretching vibration of C–H [34]. As elaborated in Fig. 2d, the peaks at 1570, 1410 and 1090 cm−1 were attributed to the CC, OC–O and C–N bonds, respectively [29]. These surface functional groups demonstrate that the aloe leaf-based N-CQDs have good hydrophilicity due to their abundant hydrophilic groups such as amino group and carboxyl group.

Using XPS analysis, the three typical peaks at 285.1, 400.1 and 532.1 eV corresponded to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively (Fig. 2e) [35]. The N-CQDs mainly consisted of carbon (60.7 %), oxygen (28.3 %), and nitrogen (6.1 %). More detailed chemical valence states of N-CQDs were obtained by using single-element-fitting HR-XPS energy spectrum. The deconvoluted C1s spectrum as shown in Fig. 2f confirmed that carbon participated in formation of the three chemical bonds, namely C–C/CC at 284.8 eV, C–N/C–O at 286.2 eV, and CO/CN at 288.4 eV [36]. The deconvoluted N1s spectrum in Fig. 2g where displayed the four peaks at 399.8 eV for pyrrolic-like N, 401.3 eV for N–H, 401.8 eV for N-(C)3 and 403.8 eV for N–O, respectively [37]. The deconvoluted spectrum of O1s indicated the two peaks at 531.4 eV for CO and 532.3 eV for C–OH/C–O–C, respectively (Fig. 2h) [35,38]. Combined with FT-IR spectrum, these convincing evidences indicate that amino, hydroxyl and carbonyl groups are rich on the surface of N-CQDs.

Based on FT-IR analysis (Fig. 3 a), the peak at 2520 cm−1, attributable to S–H stretching vibration, disappeared in that of GSH-CuNCs, which indicated formation of Cu–S bonds between GSH and CuNCs. In Fig. 3b, the peaks at 163.1, 285.1, 400.1, 531.1 and 978.1 eV corresponded to S1s, C1s, N1s, O1s, and Cu2p, respectively. As for Cu2p, two peaks at 932.0 and 951.8 eV were attributable to Cu2p3/2 and Cu2p1/2 (Fig. 3c) [39], which further confirmed the formation of Cu cores in GSH-CuNCs. In addition, the characteristic peak at ∼942 eV did not appeared in GSH-CuNCs, implying the disappearance of Cu(II) in GSH-CuNCs [40]. Since the binding energies of Cu(0) and Cu(I) differ by only 0.1 eV, the valence state of Cu cannot be precisely determined [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence properties of the aloe leaf-based N-CQDs and GSH-CuNCs
ce:section.ce:para: When the excitation wavelengths varied between 300 and 420 nm, the emission of N-CQDs exhibited a slight red-shift from 430 to 450 nm. At 350 nm, the maximum emission intensity was observed at 440 nm (Fig. 4 a). Notably, the FIs were distinctly declined when the excitation wavelengths were elevated from 350 to 420 nm. These results render us to believe that the emission property of N-CQDs was dependent on excitation wavelength, which was consistent with the previous finding [19]. By comparison, the emission wavelength of GSH-CuNCs remained constant at 660 nm under the excitation wavelengths from 300 to 350 nm. Interestingly, the GSH-CuNCs displayed the maximum emission intensity at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm (Fig. 4b). These observations provide the possibility for constructing a fluoroprobe with dual-emitting signals at one excitation wavelength, which can avoid the phenomenon that the FIs of two fluorescent materials are compromised due to their inconsistent excitation wavelengths.

The FIs of N-CQDs at 440 nm and those of CuNCs at 660 nm gradually enhanced with the rising excitation wavelengths from 300 to 350 nm. However, in the mixed system of N-CQDs/CuNCs, the 660-nm FIs declined, and the emission wavelengths (∼440 nm) of N-CQDs were red-shifted in concomitant with an enhanced FIs. Under the same excitation at 350 nm, the complex system of N-CQDs and GSH-CuNCs displayed two distinct emission peaks at 440 nm and 660 nm, respectively (Fig. 4c). These results confirm that a dual-emitting fluoroprobe can be constructed by mixing the aloe leaf-based N-CQDs with GSH-CuNCs in an appropriate proportion. In the mixed system, the FIs of N-CQDs at 440 nm were prominently quenched by GSH-CuNCs, implying occurrence of a fluorescent IFE process between two nanomaterials. According to the comparison of the QYs of QSF as a reference chemical (in 0.1 M H2SO4, QY = 54 %, λex = 350 nm), the relative QYs values of N-CQDs and GSH-CuNCs were calculated to be 21.4 % and 1.6 %, respectively. Detailed assay of QY was provided in Supporting information (SI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence stability of aloe leaf-based N-CQDs and GSH-CuNCs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the fluorescence stability, we examined the effects of several important variables on the FIs of aloe leaf-based N-CQDs, including buffer pH, NaCl concentration, ultraviolet irradiation time, and storage time at 4 °C. As can be seen from Fig. 5 a, the relatively stable FIs of N-CQDs were observed at ∼3.0 × 105 a. u. In the buffer pH range of 3.0–12.0, whereas markedly declined under strong acidic (pH < 3) and alkaline conditions (pH > 12). The above phenomenon may be attributable the fact that under an extreme pH environment, the hydrophilic groups on the surface of N-CQDs are protonated or deprotonated. Upon the addition of NaCl (0.1–1.0 M), the FIs of CQDs varied slightly (Fig. 5b), demonstrating that N-CQDs was relatively stable even under high ionic strength. Moreover, the FIs of N-CQDs only decreased by ∼9.8 % after a 120-min continuous irradiation (Fig. 5c).

Further, we examined the effects of storage time on the FIs of the individual N-CQDs, individual GSH-CuNCs, and mixed system of N-CQDs/GSH-CuNCs at 4 °C. The relative stable FIs of N-CQDs were observed during a 80-d storage (Fig. 5d), demonstrating that N-CQDs had a satisfactory photostability. Meanwhile, the GSH-CuNCs possessed stable FIs over a 11-day storage (Fig. 5e), and the mixed system of N-CQDs/CuNCs remained a steady F660/F440 value during a 48-h storage, however, the ratio values slightly declined with the extension of time (Fig. 5f). To sum up, these findings document that the mixed system of N-CQDs/CuNCs can be used as a dual-emitting fluoroprobe for the detection of target analytes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization for nitenpyram detection by the dual-emitting fluoroprobe
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve the appropriate reaction conditions for nitenpyram detection, the five important variables were optimized, including buffer pH, dosage of N-CQDs, dosage of GSH-CuNCs, along with reaction temperature and time. Because nitenpyram was unstable in alkaline solutions, the effect of buffer pH on the fluorescence response was investigated in the range of pH 3.00–7.00. When 100 μL of N-CQDs and 300 μL of GSH-CuNCs were mixed and reacted at 25 °C for 10 min, the F660/F440 values first increased with the elevating solution pH from 3.00 to 3.50 and then decreased gradually from 3.50 to 7.00 (Fig. S2a). Thus, the buffer solution was set at pH 3.50 in the following trials. Under the solution pH 3.50 and the spiked level of nitenpyram at 100 μM, the effects of the fortified dosages of N-CQDs and CuNCs were investigated in detail. With the elevating dosages of N-CQDs from 20 to 200 μL, the F660/F440 values first increased and then reached a maximum at 100 μL, whereas sharply declined with further elevating dosages from 125 to 200 μL (Fig. S2b). Thus, 100 μL of N-CQDs was selected as the optimal dosage for nitenpyram detection.

For the dosage of GSH-CuNCs, the F660/F440 values rapid increased from 100 to 300 μL and then remained at a relatively stable level (Fig. S2c). As the incubation time between the N-CQDs/CuNCs and nitenpyram increased from 4 to 10 min, the F660/F440 values rapidly increased and then remained almost stable from 10 to 14 min, whereas being sharply decreased with further prolongation of the incubation time (14–20 min), as displayed in Fig. S2(d). When the incubation temperature increased from 5 to 20 °C, the F660/F440 values sharply increased and then decreased monotonically from 20 to 35 °C (Fig. S2e). Informed by the afore-mentioned data, the optimized experimental conditions for nitenpyram detection were summarized as follows: buffer pH of 3.5, 100 μL of N-CQDs, 300 μL of GSH-CuNCs, and 10-min reaction at 20 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of the dual-emitting fluoroprobe
ce:section.ce:para: The anti-interference or selectivity of this fluoroprobe was evaluated by adding ten metal ions, four anions, and three similar NEOs pesticides to the mixed system of N-CQDs/CuNCs. As elaborated in Fig. 6 a, the F660/F440 values remained at a high level (∼0.54) in the presence of nitenpyram, while those prominently decreased as low as 0.14–0.22 following the addition of constructure similar pesticides (Fig. 6b), cations or anions. Simultaneously, nitenpyram led to the strong color variations in solution (color changes from blue to red), while no similar case occurred for other interference ions or chemicals (inset of Fig. 6a). Therefore, it can be concluded that this dual-emitting fluoroprobe is conferred to highly selective response to nitenpyram.

When spanning the nitenpyram concentrations from 0.5 to 200 μM, the fluorescence spectra of the dual-emitting fluoroprobe are shown in Fig. 6c. Under optimized conditions as described in Section 3.4, the 440-nm FIs increased with increases in the nitenpyram concentrations, while the 660-nm FIs displayed slight changes in concomitant with the significant color variations from bright blue to dark red (insert of Fig. 6c). Based on the above phenomenon, a good linear relationship (y = 0.09949 + 0.000409 x) was achieved between the ratiometric responses (F660/F440) and the nitenpyram concentrations in the range of 0.5–200 μM, with satisfactory determination coefficient (R2 = 0.9916) (Fig. 6d). The method detection limit (MDL) of nitenpyram was calculated to be 0.15 μM, according to the freedom degree multiplied by standard deviation of the detected concentration in seven blank spiked samples (nitenpyram concentration = 1.0 μM; MDL = t
n-1 × S, t 6 = 3.14) [42].

The analytical performance of this dual-emitting fluoroprobe was compared with those of the previously reported methods for nitenpyram detection. At present, there are few reports on the fluoroprobes based-on CQDs and CuNCs to detect nitenpyram. As generalized in Table 1 , the MDL (0.15 μM) of the proposed method is lower than those of electrochemical sensor based on CuNPs/N-G (2.0 μM) [26] and some fluorescence detection based on N-CQDs@SiO2 (5.65 μM) [23], and dual-walled nitrogen-rich In(III)/Tb(III)–organic framework (0.63 μM) [43], upconverted nanoparticle-porphyrin metal organic framework (0.22 μM) [44]. Especially, it supplies a wider linear range (0.5–200 μM) in contrast to other methods. Although the LC-MS/MS and dye@MOF fluoroprobe approaches gave the lower MDLs (0.37–3.0 nM) [45,46], they all required expensive instruments, as well as complicate preparation technique. To sum up, this dual-emitting fluoroprobe has a series of advantages over conventional methods, such as simple and rapid operation, easy portability, and feasibility for on-site and visual detection under field conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching mechanisms regarding the dual-emitting system by nitenpyram
ce:section.ce:para: In the N-CQDs/CuNCs based dual-emitting system, the FIs of N-CQDs were substantially quenched following the addition of nitenpyram (Fig. 7 a), while it was not the similar case for GSH-CuNCs. This phenomenon highlighted that no aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) occurred in the quenching behavior [47]. Both FRET and IFE are referred to adsorption of a quencher overlapped with the photoluminescent spectrum of a fluorescent substance, and the FIs of fluorophore could be adjusted by the addition of quencher [48]. Obviously, a spectrum overlapping phenomenon appeared between nitenpyram's UV–Vis absorption and N-CQDs’ excitation spectrum (Fig. 7b), causing the fluorescent IFE process.

To further illustrate the underlying fluorescence-quenching mechanisms regarding the dual-emitting system by nitenpyram, the quenching constant (K
sv , M−1) was calculated according to the following Stern-Volmer equation:
F 0
F
=
K
s v
[ Q ]
+ 1 =
k q
τ 0
[ Q ]
+ 1
where F0 and F are the F660/F440 values of the N-CQDs/CuNCs system in the absence and presence of nitenpyram, respectively; and [Q] is the concentration of nitenpyram (μM), kq is quenching rate, and τ0 is the fluorescence lifetime in absence of nitenpyram, 5.7595 ns. The linear relationship between F0/F values and nitenpyram concentrations (0–200 μM) was fitted by employing the Stern-Volmer equation (R2 = 0.9730). As shown in Fig. 7c, the equation was F0/F = 1 + 0.00692 [Q](μM). The K
sv value was calculated based on the slope of the fitted curve (K
sv = 6.92 × 103 M−1). The k
q value was calculated by using the formula (k
q = K
sv /τ0), 1.21 × 1012 M−1s−1. When the k
q value was greater than 2.0 × 1010 M−1s−1, indicating that the quenching process of N-CQD/CuNCs in addition of nitenpyram was static quenching [49]. In addition, the Stern-Volmer curves at different temperatures (20, 40 and 60 °C) were also plotted. As shown in Fig. S3, the slope (K
sv ) decreased slightly with increasing temperature, which is a typical curve of the static quenching [50].

As well known, the IFE process is related to the excitation-dependent reabsorption of an absorber, and thus the fluorescence lifetime of the mixed system will not be varied much [51]. As elaborated in Fig. 7d and e, the fluorescence lifetimes of N-CQDs and GSH-CuNCs did not altered significantly before and after the addition of nitenpyram. The fluorescent decay curves were fitted using a double exponential equation to calculate the fluorescent lifetimes according to the following equation:
τ =
A 1
τ 1 2
+
A 2
τ 2 2
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2
where τ represents the average lifetimes of luminescent nanomaterials (N-CQDs, CuNCs or N-CQDs/CuNCs); τ1 and τ2 represent the fitting fluorescence lifetime; A1 and A2 denotes the corresponding ratios. Before and after the addition of nitenpyram, the average fluorescence lifetimes were estimated to be 7.1715 and 7.1251 ns for N-CQDs, and 5.7395 and 5.7033 ns for GSH-CuNCs, respectively (Table S1). The fluorescence lifetime of the quenching process remained nearly unchanged, which demonstrated that the quenching process was involved in a static quenching manner. To better understand the fluorescence quenching process, the absorption behavior of N-CQDs/CuNCs in the absence and presence of nitenpyram was rigorously analyzed. As elaborated in Fig. S4a, the shape of UV–Vis absorption spectra varied before and after the addition of nitenpyram due to the formation of non-fluorescent complex [52]. The absorption peaks at 300 nm disappeared or declined with the addition of nitenpyram. These observations further substantiate the underlying fluorescence-quenching mechanism to be a static-quenching mode.

Moreover, the intensity of the electron-withdrawing groups plays a major role in the quenching process, leading to the transfer of electrons from excited N-CQDs to electron-deficient nitenpyram [53]. The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of N-CQDs could be estimated according to the empirical formula:
EHOMO = - e(Eox + 4.4)
ELUMO = - e(Ered + 4.4)
Where Eox and Ered are the oxidative and reduction potential for N-CQDs. Fig. 7g displays the cyclic voltammetry curve of the N-CQDs in the solution. The reduction potential (Ered) of N-CQDs was computed to be −1.50 eV, and ELUMO was estimated to be −2.90 eV according to the above formula.

HOMO–LUMO gap energies can be experimentally measured by a spectroscopic method. The absorption spectra were converted to Tauc plots according to the equation:
(ahv)2 = A(hv-Eg)
Where a, h, v, A, and Eg represents for absorption coefficient, Planck constant, light frequency, a constant and excited-state energy band, respectively. Fig. 7h shows the representative Tauc curve of N-CQDs. The Eg and EHOMO of N-CQDs were calculated to be 3.86 eV and −6.76 eV, respectively, according to the equation of EHOMO = ELUMO – Eg.

Quantum chemical calculations were performed with the Gaussian 03 software package. According to the hybrid exchange correlation functional (B3LYP) method in Dmol3 mode (Fig. 7i), the EHOMO and ELUMO of nitenpyram can be calculated to be −6.20 eV and −1.55eV, respectively [43]. The LUMO energy of nitenpyram was relatively higher than the HOMO of N-CQDs, resulting in the electrons not transferring from the HOMO of N-CQDs to the LUMO of the excited state of nitenpyram.

Using FT-IR analysis, a new peak at 3741 cm−1 was observed to form in the presence of nitenpyram, and the peak intensity at 1705 cm−1 declined (Fig. 7f), implying the fracture of CO bond. In addition, several new peaks at 1027-1307 cm−1 appeared, further demonstrating the fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs induced by nitenpyram. With the elevating nitenpyram concentrations, the 440-nm FIs of N-CQDs prominently declined, while it was not the similar case for the 660-nm FIs of GSH-CuNCs.

Chen et al. (2017) concluded that owing to containing electron withdrawing functional groups, the nitro and imino moieties of nitenpyram would be protonated in acidic media, making it positively charged [54]. Both the zeta potentials of N-CQDs and GSH-CuNCs were negatively charged, and the absolute potential value of GSH-CuNCs was significantly higher than that of N-CQDs. By comparison, the absolute potential value of N-CQDs/CuNCs in the addition of nitenpyram was significantly reduced than those of the individual N-CQDs, CuNCs or their mixed systems (Fig. S4b), corroborating that the charges of the dual-emitting system became neutralized upon the addition of nitenpyram. To sum up, the fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs by nitenpyram was attributable the joint principles of IFE and static quenching effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real samples analysis by the dual-emitting fluoroprobe
ce:section.ce:para: The N-CQDs/CuNCs based fluoroprobe was used to detect nitenpyram in real-world waters, including the four environmental waters and two effluents from sewage treatment plant. As generalized in Table 2 , nitenpyram was not detected in all waters, indicating below its MDL (0.15 μM). The fortified recoveries for nitenpyram ranged from 95.0 % to 107.0 % at three spiked levels (1, 5 and 10 μM) with the relative standard deviations (RSDs) of 1.4%–2.5 %. Compared to the detection results by traditional LC-MS/MS method, the dual-emitting fluoroprobe offered inexpensive cost and similar recoveries for nitenpyram in six water samples. The LC-MS/MS profiles in the unfortified and fortified water samples are shown in Fig. S5, and the pretreatment method and underlying detection parameters, including product mass, collision energy, and tube lens offset are described in Supplementary information and listed in Table S2. The intra-day and intra-day precisions (RSDs) were 1.1%–2.6 % and 1.4%–3.7 %, respectively according to the six parallel experiments (n = 6) (Table S3). These results demonstrate that the dual-emitting fluoroprobe based on the N-CQDs/CuNCs mixed system is of high accuracy and precision for nitenpyram detection in real-world waters.

Herein, we successfully fabricated the N-CQDs using Aloe carazo leaves as carbon source and urea as nitrogen source, respectively. When the biomass N-CQDs were mixed with GSH-CuNCs, the resulting mixed system produced dual-emitting signals at 440 and 660 nm at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Upon the addition of nitenpyram, the FIs of aloe leaf-based N-CQDs significantly decreased, whereas those of GSH-CuNCs remained nearly constant, in concomitant with a distinct color change from bright blue to dark red. The F660/F440 values presented a good linear relationship with varying nitenpyram concentrations from 0.5 to 200 μM, thereby yielding a MDL of 0.15 μM. In addition, the N-CQDs/GSH-CuNCs based dual-emitting fluoroprobe supplied satisfactory fortification recoveries (95.0%–107.0 %) for nitenpyram with RSDs of 1.4%–2.5 %, which were superior to the traditional LC-MS/MS method. Notably, the newly developed fluoroprobe has the advantages of simple and convenient operations, low-cost preparation, environmental friendliness, no requirement for expensive instrumentation, and feasibility for on-site monitoring under field conditions.
